next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 6

Nate Griggs had been holding his phone toward the basement ceiling for three minutes, tilting it at different angles like a man trying to divine water from a satellite that had better things to do. The search page loaded in pieces: Google logo first, then a white void, then the top result emerging letter by letter like a telegram from someone who wanted him to suffer.

*Can police recover deleted voicemails from a phone*

The first result was a legal advice subreddit, which was exactly the kind of source a serious person would not consult and exactly the kind of source Nate was going to read with the focus of a man studying his own biopsy results. He thumbed it open. The page loaded in strips, each paragraph arriving on its own schedule.

*Depends on carrier. Verizon keeps metadata (call time, duration, number) for 1-2 years. Actual voicemail audio is generally retained on their servers for 30-40 days after the user deletes it. Law enforcement can access recordings through a subpoena or court order.*

Thirty to forty days. He was on day one.

Nate sat down on the pull-out sofa. The mattress was thin enough that the metal frame bit into the backs of his thighs through the fabric. Doug's duffel bag sat on the other pull-out, zipped tight as a vacuum seal. Doug packed like a man trying to prove he took up no space in the world. The lake was audible through the small casement windows, a steady lapping that Nate's brain, helpfully, converted into a countdown.

The voicemail. The goddamn voicemail.

He'd left it around eight on Friday evening, after the draft was don...
open_hooks_to_carry: Paul corrects Mallick about the body's position — revealing he knows how it was oriented
Cliff corrects her about which room is Martin's study — revealing he was in there
Doug barely speaks in his pre-interview moment, and Mallick notes the silence
Mallick's private assessment: five men who claim they slept through the night, and the house was rearranged by morning
Each interview should feel distinct — Paul's insurance precision, Cliff's sales smoothness, Doug's monosyllables
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ut to the deck. What kept him there was Karen, and Martin refusing on Thursday night to deny the one thing Joel had driven up to ask him. It was the Panera in Edina, Karen with both hands around a paper cup, saying she wanted something different. It was the month since, every little detail in his life quietly rearranging itself into a shape he hated. Martin died before Joel decided whether he wanted an apology, a confession, or just the chance to hit him.

The coffee in his mug had gone cold. Joel drank it anyway. It tasted like nothing.

"I'm going to sit on the deck," he said.

Nobody stopped him.

Outside, September air came off the lake cool enough that his breath almost showed. Low sun laid gold across the water. Loons called somewhere past the dock. Martin's house. Martin's lake. Martin's weekend, still here without Martin in it.

A beer bottle sat on the railing from last night, its label peeled halfway off where Tommy had worried at it while he talked, thick fingers working the paper while his mouth kept moving. Warm stale beer clung to the glass. Joel stood there looking at it and thinking about how normal last night had felt while it was happening, which meant it would never feel normal again.

From inside, through the screen door, Mallick's footsteps started down the stairs. A moment later her voice carried into the kitchen, warm and chatty, asking one of the deputies about the cedar paneling in the hallway.

Joel leaned on the railing and watched the lake and thought: she's either an idiot or the most dangerous person in this house, and I still can't tell which.
secondary_character_beats: Mallick's domestic details (mentioning the casserole warming, asking about coffee) should function as disarming tools, not character tics
state_deltas_required: Mallick's interviews exposing more than the interviewees intend
Mallick knows the scene was tampered with but not by how many people or why
